Multiple traces


No Reinstate CMR
New trace, no context blend
Distinct traces


Reinforcement CMR
New trace + boost first
First trace strengthened

title: Reinforcement Positional CMR
text:
Theoretical Connections
This model relates to:

Encoding variability theory: Each presentation creates a new trace
Remindings hypothesis: Re-seeing something triggers memory of the original
Strength-based models: Repetition increases trace strength

The key insight is that reinforcement targets the original context, not the current context—connecting the repetition back to its first occurrence.

title: Outlist CMR (CMR-DE)
text: Outlist CMR (also called CMR-DE, “Distinct Encoding”) provides an alternative approach to handling repeated items. Instead of using position-based encoding, it maintains item-based encoding but introduces out-of-list context to distinguish presentations.

title: Outlist CMR (CMR-DE)
text:
The Mechanism
When an item appears for the second time, the model mixes its contextual input with an “out-of-list” context unit. This creates temporal separation between the two presentations:

First presentation: Normal context integration
Repetition: Context input mixed with a unique out-of-list unit

The out-of-list context makes the second presentation’s trace partially distinct from the first.

title: Outlist CMR (CMR-DE)
text:
Why Out-of-List Context?
The theoretical motivation is that seeing something familiar triggers a different mental state than seeing something new:

“I’ve seen this before” is itself part of the encoding context
The repetition carries information about being a repetition
This meta-cognitive state affects how the item is encoded

title: Outlist CMR (CMR-DE)
text:
Mathematical Specification

First Presentation
Standard encoding: \[\mathbf{c}^{IN}_i = M^{FC} \mathbf{f}_i\] \[\mathbf{c}_{new} = \rho \mathbf{c}_{old} + \beta_{enc} \mathbf{c}^{IN}_i\]


Repetition (item already encoded)
Mixed context input: \[\mathbf{c}^{mix} = \alpha \cdot \mathbf{c}^{IN}_i + (1-\alpha) \cdot \mathbf{c}^{out}\]
where: - \(\mathbf{c}^{IN}_i = M^{FC} \mathbf{f}_i\) (item’s normal context) - \(\mathbf{c}^{out}\) = next available out-of-list context unit - \(\alpha\) = mfc_trace_sensitivity (balance parameter)
Then integrate: \[\mathbf{c}_{new} = \rho \mathbf{c}_{old} + \beta_{enc} \mathbf{c}^{mix}\]


Expanded Context Space
To support out-of-list context, the context vector is expanded:

Standard size: \(N + 1\) (items + start-of-list)
Expanded size: \(2N + 1\) (items + out-of-list + start-of-list)

Each repetition consumes a new out-of-list unit.

title: Outlist CMR (CMR-DE)
text:
Parameters








Parameter
Symbol
Description




mfc_trace_sensitivity
\(\alpha\)
Balance between item context and out-of-list context




\(\alpha = 1.0\): No out-of-list mixing (equivalent to standard CMR)
\(\alpha = 0.5\): Equal mix of item and out-of-list context
\(\alpha = 0.0\): Only out-of-list context (complete distinction)

title: Outlist CMR (CMR-DE)
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.models.outlist_cmr import CMR
import jaxcmr.components.context as TemporalContext

params = {
    "encoding_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "start_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "recall_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "learning_rate": 0.5,
    "primacy_scale": 2.0,
    "primacy_decay": 0.8,
    "shared_support": 0.05,
    "item_support": 0.25,
    "choice_sensitivity": 0.6,
    "mfc_trace_sensitivity": 0.7,  # Out-of-list mixing ratio
    "stop_probability_scale": 0.05,
    "stop_probability_growth": 0.2,
    "learn_after_context_update": True,
    "allow_repeated_recalls": False,
}

# Must use expanded context
model = CMR(
    list_length=16,
    parameters=params,
    context_create_fn=TemporalContext.init_expanded,  # Required!
)

title: Outlist CMR (CMR-DE)
text:
Comparison: Positional vs Outlist








Aspect
Positional CMR
Outlist CMR




Feature layer
Position-based
Item-based


Distinction mechanism
Different position = different trace
Out-of-list context unit


Memory structure
Positions in MCF
Items in MCF (standard)


Context size
Standard
Expanded (2N+1)


Recall identifies
Position
Item

title: Outlist CMR (CMR-DE)
text:
Predictions

Context Similarity
With \(\alpha < 1\): - First and second presentations have partially distinct contexts - Lower \(\alpha\) = more distinct - Transitions from repetition don’t strongly cue first presentation


Spacing Effects
The out-of-list mixing happens regardless of spacing. This differs from drift-based models where spacing affects context overlap directly.

title: Outlist CMR (CMR-DE)
text:
Implementation
Key code from experience_item:
def experience_item(self, item_index):
    item = self.items[item_index]
    context_input = self.mfc.probe(item)

    # Check if item was already encoded
    new_context = lax.cond(
        self.recallable[item_index],  # Already studied?
        # Repetition: mix with out-of-list context
        lambda: self.context.integrate_with_outlist(
            context_input, self.mfc_sensitivity, self.encoding_drift_rate
        ),
        # First presentation: standard integration
        lambda: self.context.integrate(context_input, self.encoding_drift_rate),
    )
    ...
The integrate_with_outlist method in TemporalContext handles the mixing:
def integrate_with_outlist(self, inlist_input, ratio, drift_rate):
    # Mix in-list input with next available out-of-list unit
    context_input = normalize_magnitude(
        (normalize_magnitude(inlist_input) * ratio)
        + (self.outlist_input * (1 - ratio))
    )
    # Then standard integration...

title: Outlist CMR (CMR-DE)
text:
Theoretical Connections
This model relates to:

Encoding specificity: Each encoding event has unique contextual features
Global matching models: Items can match based on overall similarity, not just local context
Recognition memory: The “old” signal at repetition affects encoding

The out-of-list context can be interpreted as an “already seen” tag that becomes part of the memory trace.

title: Drift Positional CMR
text: Drift Positional CMR extends Positional CMR with a separate context drift rate for repeated presentations. When an item appears for the second (or later) time, context drifts at a different rate than for first presentations.

title: Drift Positional CMR
text:
The Mechanism
In standard encoding, context drifts at rate \(\beta_{enc}\) for all items. Drift Positional CMR uses:
\[\beta_{item} = \begin{cases}
\beta_{enc} & \text{if first presentation} \\
\beta_{rep} & \text{if repetition}
\end{cases}\]
This allows the model to capture phenomena where repetitions have different contextual effects than first presentations.

title: Drift Positional CMR
text:
Why Different Drift?
Several theoretical motivations exist for repetition-specific drift:

Reduced novelty: Repeated items may update context less because they’re already somewhat familiar
Chunking: Repetitions might integrate more strongly, linking the two presentations
Attention differences: People may attend differently to repeated items

title: Drift Positional CMR
text:
Mathematical Specification

Encoding Decision
When item \(k\) is presented at position \(i\):
if item_k ∈ studied[0:i]:
    drift_rate = β_rep  # Repetition
else:
    drift_rate = β_enc  # First presentation


Context Update
\[\mathbf{c}_i = \rho_i \mathbf{c}_{i-1} + \beta_{item} \mathbf{c}^{IN}_i\]
where \(\beta_{item}\) is determined by the check above.

title: Drift Positional CMR
text:
Parameters








Parameter
Symbol
Description




encoding_drift_rate
\(\beta_{enc}\)
Drift rate for first presentations


repetition_drift_rate
\(\beta_{rep}\)
Drift rate for repetitions



All other parameters are inherited from Positional CMR.

title: Drift Positional CMR
text:
Predictions







\(\beta_{rep}\)
Interpretation




\(< \beta_{enc}\)
Repetitions update context less (reduced novelty)


\(= \beta_{enc}\)
Equivalent to standard Positional CMR


\(> \beta_{enc}\)
Repetitions update context more (enhanced chunking)


\(= 0\)
Repetitions don’t update context at all


\(= 1\)
Repetitions completely reset context

title: Drift Positional CMR
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.models.drift_positional_cmr import CMR

params = {
    "encoding_drift_rate": 0.6,      # First presentations
    "repetition_drift_rate": 0.3,    # Repetitions (less drift)
    "start_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "recall_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "learning_rate": 0.5,
    "primacy_scale": 2.0,
    "primacy_decay": 0.8,
    "shared_support": 0.05,
    "item_support": 0.25,
    "choice_sensitivity": 0.6,
    "mfc_sensitivity": 3.0,
    "stop_probability_scale": 0.05,
    "stop_probability_growth": 0.2,
    "learn_after_context_update": True,
    "allow_repeated_recalls": False,
}

model = CMR(list_length=16, parameters=params)

title: Drift Positional CMR
text:
Experimental Predictions

Spacing Effects
With \(\beta_{rep} < \beta_{enc}\):

Massed repetitions (immediate): Second presentation barely shifts context → strong overlap with first
Spaced repetitions (delayed): Context has drifted far; repetition at lower rate creates moderate overlap

This can produce the classic spacing effect: spaced repetitions are better remembered than massed repetitions.


Lag-Recency Analysis
The model predicts different lag-CRP curves for: - Transitions from first presentations - Transitions from repetitions (if \(\beta_{rep} \neq \beta_{enc}\))
Lower \(\beta_{rep}\) would produce flatter lag-CRP for repetitions (less context change = less contiguity).

title: Drift Positional CMR
text:
When To Use
Use Drift Positional CMR when:

Fitting data with repeated items
Spacing effects are important
You hypothesize that repetitions affect context differently

title: Drift Positional CMR
text:
Implementation
Key difference from Positional CMR:
def experience_item(self, item_index):
    # Check if item has been studied before
    is_repetition = jnp.isin(item_index + 1, self.studied)

    # Use appropriate drift rate
    new_context = lax.cond(
        is_repetition,
        lambda: self.context.integrate(context_input, self.repetition_drift_rate),
        lambda: self.context.integrate(context_input, self.encoding_drift_rate),
    )
    ...

title: Drift Positional CMR
text:
Relationship to Other Variants



Variant
Key Mechanism




Positional CMR
Position-based encoding


Drift Positional CMR
Position + different drift for repetitions


Reinforcement CMR
Position + first-presentation boosting


Outlist CMR
Item-based + out-of-list context

title: Blend Positional CMR
text: Blend Positional CMR maintains two parallel context streams: one item-based (like standard CMR) and one position-based (like Positional CMR). A blend parameter controls their relative contribution to recall competition.

title: Blend Positional CMR
text:
The Problem It Solves
Pure positional CMR predicts that repeated items have completely distinct traces. Pure item-based CMR predicts complete context blending. Empirically, the truth lies between:

Erroneous transitions to neighbors of both presentations occur
But second-presentation neighbors are cued less than pure CMR predicts

Blend CMR captures this intermediate pattern.

title: Blend Positional CMR
text:
Mathematical Specification

Encoding
Each study event updates both streams:
Position stream: \[\mathbf{c}^{pos}_{new} = \rho \mathbf{c}^{pos}_{old} + \beta_{enc} M^{FC}_{pos} \mathbf{p}_i\]
Item stream: \[\mathbf{c}^{item}_{new} = \rho \mathbf{c}^{item}_{old} + \beta_{enc} M^{FC}_{item} \mathbf{f}_k\]
Both MFC and MCF are updated for each stream with their respective representations.


Retrieval: Blending Activations

Get raw activations from each stream:

Position stream (per position): \[a^{pos}_i = (M^{CF}_{pos} \mathbf{c}^{pos})_i \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{recallable}[i]]\]
Item stream (per item, mapped to positions): \[a^{item}_k = (M^{CF}_{item} \mathbf{c}^{item})_k\] \[a^{item}_{pos[i]} = a^{item}_{\text{studied}[i]}\]

Normalize each stream: \[p^{pos}_i = \frac{a^{pos}_i}{\sum_j a^{pos}_j}\] \[p^{item}_i = \frac{a^{item}_{pos[i]}}{\sum_j a^{item}_{pos[j]}}\]
Blend: \[p^{blend}_i = (1 - w) \cdot p^{pos}_i + w \cdot p^{item}_i\]

where \(w\) = blend_weight.

Apply choice sensitivity: \[a^{final}_i = (p^{blend}_i)^\tau\]

title: Blend Positional CMR
text:
The Blend Weight Parameter



blend_weight
Behavior




0.0
Pure positional (distinct traces)


0.5
Equal contribution from both


1.0
Pure item-based (shared context)

title: Blend Positional CMR
text:
Predictions

Transitions After Recalling Repeated Item
With \(w\) between 0 and 1: - Neighbors of first presentation: High probability (position stream) - Neighbors of second presentation: Moderate probability (item stream contributes)
The item stream provides context that was present at all presentations, linking repetitions together.


Why Two Streams?
The position stream captures: - Each presentation had a distinct temporal context - Recall can target specific presentations
The item stream captures: - The item identity persisted across presentations - Some context is shared (semantic, perceptual)

title: Blend Positional CMR
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.models.blend_positional_cmr import CMR

params = {
    "encoding_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "start_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "recall_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "learning_rate": 0.5,
    "primacy_scale": 2.0,
    "primacy_decay": 0.8,
    "shared_support": 0.05,
    "item_support": 0.25,
    "choice_sensitivity": 0.6,
    "mfc_sensitivity": 3.0,
    "blend_weight": 0.3,  # 30% item-based, 70% positional
    "stop_probability_scale": 0.05,
    "stop_probability_growth": 0.2,
    "learn_after_context_update": True,
    "allow_repeated_recalls": False,
}

model = CMR(list_length=16, parameters=params)

title: Blend Positional CMR
text:
Implementation Architecture
class CMR(Pytree):
    def __init__(self, ...):
        # Position-based stream (Positional CMR style)
        self.position_context = context_create_fn(list_length)
        self.position_mfc = mfc_create_fn(...)
        self.position_mcf = mcf_create_fn(...)

        # Item-based stream (Standard CMR style)
        self.item_context = context_create_fn(list_length)
        self.item_mfc = mfc_create_fn(...)
        self.item_mcf = mcf_create_fn(...)

        # Blend parameter
        self.blend_weight = parameters["blend_weight"]

    def position_activations(self):
        # Get activations from both streams
        raw_pos = self._raw_position_stream()
        raw_item = self._raw_item_stream()

        # Normalize to probabilities
        pos_prob = raw_pos / sum(raw_pos)
        item_prob = raw_item / sum(raw_item)

        # Blend
        blended = (1 - self.blend_weight) * pos_prob
                + self.blend_weight * item_prob

        # Apply sensitivity
        return power_scale(blended, self.mcf_sensitivity)

title: Blend Positional CMR
text:
When To Use
Use Blend Positional CMR when:

Testing intermediate levels of context sharing
Neither pure positional nor pure item-based fits well
The data shows partial links between repetitions

title: Blend Positional CMR
text:
Computational Cost
Blend CMR maintains twice the memory structures of standard CMR: - Two context vectors - Two MFC matrices - Two MCF matrices
This roughly doubles memory and computation.

title: Memory Systems
text: CMR uses two memory systems that map between items and context. jaxcmr provides two implementations: linear associative memory (the default) and instance-based memory (MINERVA-2 style). These produce similar behavior in standard paradigms but differ in how they handle repeated items.

title: Memory Systems
text:
The Two Memory Systems
CMR requires two directional memories:








Memory
Direction
Function




MFC
Item → Context
Retrieves contextual input when an item is presented or recalled


MCF
Context → Item
Computes item activations given the current context



Both memories start with pre-experimental associations and acquire new associations during study.

title: Memory Systems
text:
Linear Associative Memory
The default implementation stores associations as a weight matrix.

Learning
When item \(\mathbf{f}\) and context \(\mathbf{c}\) are associated with learning rate \(\eta\):
\[\Delta W_{ij} = \eta \cdot f_i \cdot c_j\]
This is a Hebbian outer product—co-active units strengthen their connection.


Retrieval
Given a cue \(\mathbf{x}\), the output is a linear combination:
\[\mathbf{y} = W \cdot \mathbf{x}\]


Properties

Blends at storage: Multiple associations to the same output overlap in the weight matrix
Efficient: Retrieval is a single matrix-vector product
Lossy: Individual episodes cannot be perfectly separated



MFC Initialization
def init_mfc(list_length, parameters, context):
    # Pre-experimental: each item → unique context unit
    # Weight = 1 - learning_rate (room for new associations)
    return LinearMemory(
        jnp.eye(item_count, context_size, 1) * (1 - learning_rate)
    )
The offset of 1 skips the start-of-list unit (index 0), so item 1 maps to context unit 1.


MCF Initialization
def init_mcf(list_length, parameters, context):
    # Pre-experimental associations
    # shared_support: all context units → all items
    # item_support: each context unit → its item (extra)
    base = jnp.full((context_size - 1, item_count), shared_support)
    for i in range(item_count):
        base[i, i] = item_support
    # Start-of-list unit (index 0) has no associations
    return LinearMemory(jnp.vstack((zeros, base)))

title: Memory Systems
text:
Instance-Based Memory
An alternative implementation inspired by MINERVA 2 (Hintzman, 1984) stores each association as a separate trace.

Learning
When item \(\mathbf{f}\) and context \(\mathbf{c}\) are associated:
\[M_{trace_i} = [\mathbf{f}, \gamma \cdot \mathbf{c}]\]
Each study event creates a new row in the trace matrix. The learning rate \(\gamma\) scales the context portion.


Retrieval
Given a cue (partial probe), each trace is activated by its similarity:
\[A_i = (\text{cue} \cdot m_i)^\tau\]
The parameter \(\tau\) (trace selectivity) controls the sharpness of matching:

\(\tau = 1\): Linear similarity, gentle competition
\(\tau > 1\): Superlinear, strong matches dominate
\(\tau \to \infty\): Winner-take-all

The output is the weighted sum of traces:
\[\mathbf{echo} = \sum_i A_i \cdot m_i\]


Properties

Preserves episodes: Each association is stored separately
Flexible retrieval: \(\tau\) controls whether memories blend or compete
More traces = more computation: Scales with number of study events



Mathematical Comparison








Aspect
Linear
Instance




Storage
Matrix \(W\)
Trace matrix \(M\)


Learning
\(W \leftarrow W + \eta \mathbf{f} \mathbf{c}^T\)
\(M \leftarrow [M; \mathbf{f}, \gamma\mathbf{c}]\)


Retrieval
\(W\mathbf{x}\)
\(\sum_i (\mathbf{x} \cdot m_i)^\tau m_i\)


Episodes
Blended
Preserved


Parameters
—
\(\tau\) (trace selectivity)

title: Memory Systems
text:
When Does It Matter?
For single-presentation paradigms (each item studied once), both implementations produce similar behavior. Differences emerge with:

Repeated Items
When an item is presented twice:

Linear: Both presentations strengthen the same associations (additive)
Instance: Two separate traces exist; retrieval depends on which trace is activated

Instance memory allows selective retrieval of specific presentations, useful for: - Spacing effects - Source memory - Context-dependent recall of repetitions


Interference Paradigms
When new associations must override old ones:

Linear: Old associations persist (interference)
Instance: New traces can dominate with high \(\tau\)

title: Memory Systems
Memory Systems
text:
Choosing a Memory System



Use Case
Recommended




Standard free recall
LinearMemory (simpler, faster)


Repetition paradigms
InstanceMemory (preserves episodes)


Spacing effects
InstanceMemory


Source memory
InstanceMemory


Efficient fitting
LinearMemory

title: Memory Systems
text:
Usage
Specify memory systems when creating a model:
from jaxcmr.models.cmr import CMR
import jaxcmr.components.linear_memory as LinearMemory
import jaxcmr.components.instance_memory as InstanceMemory

# Default: linear memory
model = CMR(
    list_length=16,
    parameters=params,
    mfc_create_fn=LinearMemory.init_mfc,
    mcf_create_fn=LinearMemory.init_mcf,
)

# Instance-based memory
params["mcf_trace_sensitivity"] = 3.0  # τ parameter
params["mfc_trace_sensitivity"] = 1.0

model = CMR(
    list_length=16,
    parameters=params,
    mfc_create_fn=InstanceMemory.init_mfc,
    mcf_create_fn=InstanceMemory.init_mcf,
)

title: Memory Systems
text:
Additional Parameters for Instance Memory



Parameter
Description




mcf_trace_sensitivity
\(\tau\) for context→item retrieval


mfc_trace_sensitivity
\(\tau\) for item→context retrieval



Higher values increase selectivity; traces with high similarity dominate. Lower values blend more traces.

title: Memory Systems
text:
Theoretical Connections
The linear vs. instance distinction connects to broader debates in memory theory:

Connectionist models (e.g., back-propagation networks) use distributed representations like linear associative memory
Multiple-trace models (e.g., MINERVA, REM) store separate episodes like instance memory

For CMR, the choice affects how repetitions and interference are handled without changing the core temporal context mechanism.

title: Memory Systems
text:
References

Anderson, J. A. (1972). A simple neural network generating an interactive memory. Mathematical Biosciences, 14, 197-220.
Hintzman, D. L. (1984). MINERVA 2: A simulation model of human memory. Behavior Research Methods, Instruments, & Computers, 16(2), 96-101.
Turner, B. M., et al. (2019). Approaching human performance in the generalized learning from feedback paradigm using linear and instance-based models. Computational Brain & Behavior, 2, 187-207.

title: Temporal Context
text: Temporal context is the core representation in CMR that makes memory “episodic”—it binds items to when they occurred, not just what they are.

title: Temporal Context
text:
What Is Temporal Context?
Temporal context is a vector that drifts as items are experienced. Think of it as a summary of recent mental activity that:

Changes gradually — each new item shifts context, but doesn’t reset it
Stays unit-length — normalization ensures consistent magnitude
Preserves history — earlier items leave traces in current context

This produces a natural recency gradient: recent items share more context with the current state than distant items.

title: Temporal Context
text:
Mathematical Specification

Representation
Context is a unit-length vector of size \(N+1\) where \(N\) is the list length:
\[\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{N+1}, \quad \|\mathbf{c}\| = 1\]
The extra dimension (index 0) represents the “start-of-list” context.


Initialization
At the start of the experiment:
\[\mathbf{c}_0 = [1, 0, 0, \ldots, 0]^T\]
Only the start-of-list unit is active. This state is preserved as initial_state to enable drift back at retrieval onset.


Context Drift (Integration)
When a new input \(\mathbf{c}^{IN}\) arrives with drift rate \(\beta\):
\[\mathbf{c}_{new} = \rho \mathbf{c}_{old} + \beta \mathbf{c}^{IN}\]
The scaling factor \(\rho\) ensures the result remains unit-length:
\[\rho = \sqrt{1 + \beta^2\left[(\mathbf{c}_{old} \cdot \mathbf{c}^{IN})^2 - 1\right]} - \beta(\mathbf{c}_{old} \cdot \mathbf{c}^{IN})\]


Input Normalization
Before integration, inputs are normalized:
\[\mathbf{c}^{IN} \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{c}^{IN}}{\|\mathbf{c}^{IN}\|}\]
This ensures consistent drift behavior regardless of input magnitude.

title: Temporal Context
text:
Drift Rate Effects
The drift rate \(\beta\) controls how much context changes with each input:



\(\beta\) Value
Effect




0.0
No change (context frozen)


0.3
Slow drift, high similarity between adjacent items


0.6
Moderate drift, balanced temporal precision


0.9
Fast drift, each item has distinct context


1.0
Complete replacement (only new input matters)



Lower drift rates create broader temporal “windows”—items studied close together share similar contexts. Higher rates create sharper distinctions.

title: Temporal Context
text:
Context During Study
During encoding, each item \(i\) produces a contextual input through the MFC memory:
\[\mathbf{c}^{IN}_i = M^{FC} \mathbf{f}_i\]
where \(\mathbf{f}_i\) is the item’s feature representation. Initially, this retrieves the item’s pre-experimental context unit. As associations form, it retrieves a blend of item-specific and experimentally-learned context.
The context then drifts:
\[\mathbf{c}_i = \rho_i \mathbf{c}_{i-1} + \beta_{enc} \mathbf{c}^{IN}_i\]

title: Temporal Context
text:
Context During Retrieval

Start of Retrieval
Before the first recall, context drifts toward the start-of-list state:
\[\mathbf{c}_{start} = \rho_{N+1} \mathbf{c}_N + \beta_{start} \mathbf{c}_0\]
This balances recency (from \(\mathbf{c}_N\)) with primacy (from \(\mathbf{c}_0\)):

High \(\beta_{start}\) → strong primacy, first items cued
Low \(\beta_{start}\) → strong recency, last items cued



After Each Recall
When item \(i\) is recalled, its context is reinstated:
\[\mathbf{c}^{IN} = M^{FC} \mathbf{f}_i\]
This retrieved context contains experimentally-learned associations—it resembles the context from when item \(i\) was studied. Context then drifts:
\[\mathbf{c}_{new} = \rho \mathbf{c}_{old} + \beta_{rec} \mathbf{c}^{IN}\]
This context reinstatement is the key to contiguity: items studied near item \(i\) had similar contexts, so they receive high activation when cued.

title: Temporal Context
text:
Implementation
from jaxcmr.components.context import TemporalContext, init

# Create context for a 16-item list
context = init(item_count=16)

# Context has 17 dimensions (16 items + start-of-list)
print(context.size)  # 17

# Initial state: only start-of-list active
print(context.state)  # [1, 0, 0, ..., 0]

# Integrate a new input
import jax.numpy as jnp
context_input = jnp.zeros(17).at[1].set(1.0)  # First item's context unit
new_context = context.integrate(context_input, drift_rate=0.5)

# State has shifted toward the input while staying unit-length
print(jnp.linalg.norm(new_context.state))  # 1.0

title: Temporal Context
Temporal Context
text:
Expanded Context
For models needing out-of-list context (e.g., for repetition paradigms), use init_expanded:
from jaxcmr.components.context import TemporalContext

# Create context with extra capacity for out-of-list units
context = TemporalContext.init_expanded(item_count=16)
print(context.size)  # 33 (16 items + 16 outlist + 1 start)

# Get out-of-list input
outlist_input = context.outlist_input

# Integrate mixed in-list and out-of-list context
new_context = context.integrate_with_outlist(
    inlist_input=some_input,
    ratio=0.7,  # 70% in-list, 30% out-of-list
    drift_rate=0.5,
)

title: Temporal Context
text:
Why Unit-Length?
Maintaining unit-length context serves two purposes:

Consistent retrieval strength — The dot product \(M^{CF} \mathbf{c}\) has bounded magnitude
Interpretable similarity — \(\mathbf{c}_1 \cdot \mathbf{c}_2\) directly measures context similarity

Without normalization, activations would grow unboundedly as context accumulates history.

title: Temporal Context
text:
Visualizing Context Drift
The TemporalContext class includes visualization support in Jupyter:
context._repr_markdown_()  # Returns HTML img tag of heatmap
This shows the current context state as a heatmap, useful for debugging and understanding model behavior.

title: Temporal Context
text:
References

Howard, M. W., & Kahana, M. J. (2002). A distributed representation of temporal context. Journal of Mathematical Psychology, 46, 269-299.
Sederberg, P. B., Howard, M. W., & Kahana, M. J. (2008). A context-based theory of recency and contiguity in free recall. Psychological Review, 115(4), 893-912.

title: Conditional Response Probability
text:
The Conditional Response Probability (CRP) curve shows the probability of recalling an item as a function of its lag (study position relative to the just-recalled item).
Key features:

Temporal contiguity: Items studied nearby tend to be recalled together
Forward asymmetry: Transitions to +1 lag are more likely than -1
Peak at lag +1 and -1

title: Conditional Response Probability
text:
The Conditional Response Probability (CRP) curve shows the probability of recalling an item as a function of its lag (study position relative to the just-recalled item).
Key features:

Temporal contiguity: Items studied nearby tend to be recalled together
Forward asymmetry: Transitions to +1 lag are more likely than -1
Peak at lag +1 and -1

title: Conditional Response Probability
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.analyses.crp import crp, plot_crp

# Compute CRP
result = crp(data["recalls"], list_length=16)

# Plot comparing model and data
plot_crp(
    datasets=[simulation, data],
    labels=["Model", "Data"],
)

title: Conditional Response Probability
text:
Interpretation



Pattern
Interpretation




Sharp peak at ±1
Strong temporal contiguity


Forward asymmetry
Context drift creates forward bias


Flat curve
Random transitions

title: Probability of Nth Recall
text:
Probability of Nth Recall (PNR) shows which study positions are most likely to be recalled at each output position (1st recall, 2nd recall, etc.).
This reveals:

Where recall typically starts (usually recency)
How recall transitions across the list
Differences in early vs. late output positions

title: Importing Data
text: Before computing analyses, you need to load your data into the RecallDataset format.

title: Importing Data
text:
From HDF5
The standard format for jaxcmr data:
import h5py
import jax.numpy as jnp

def load_dataset(path: str) -> dict:
    """Load a RecallDataset from HDF5."""
    with h5py.File(path, "r") as f:
        return {key: jnp.array(f[key][:]) for key in f.keys()}

dataset = load_dataset("experiment.h5")

title: Importing Data
text:
Validating Data
Check your dataset has the required fields:
required = ["subject", "listLength", "pres_itemnos", "recalls"]
for field in required:
    assert field in dataset, f"Missing required field: {field}"
See Data Format for complete field specifications.

title: Intrusion Errors
text:
Intrusion errors occur when participants recall items that were not on the study list. These can be:

Prior-list intrusions (items from previous lists)
Extra-experimental intrusions (items never studied)

title: Concepts
text: This section covers the foundational concepts you need to understand jaxcmr.

title: Concepts
text:
What is jaxcmr?
jaxcmr is a JAX-accelerated toolkit for computational memory research. It provides:

Analyses: Functions for computing behavioral measures from recall data
Models: Implementations of CMR and related memory search models
Evaluation: Tools for fitting models to data and comparing them

title: Concepts
text:
Core Design
The library is built around typed protocols that define extension points. Implement these protocols and your code integrates with the entire ecosystem.

title: Concepts
text:
Sections

Why JAX - JAX fundamentals for memory science
Data Format - The RecallDataset structure
Architecture - How models are composed
Evaluation Pipeline - Fitting and simulation flow

title: Data Format
text: jaxcmr expects data in a specific format defined by the RecallDataset TypedDict.

title: Data Format
text:
RecallDataset Structure
class RecallDataset(TypedDict):
    # Required fields
    subject: Integer[Array, "n_trials 1"]
    listLength: Integer[Array, "n_trials 1"]
    pres_itemnos: Integer[Array, "n_trials num_presented"]
    recalls: Integer[Array, "n_trials num_recalled"]

    # Optional fields
    pres_itemids: Integer[Array, "n_trials num_presented"]
    rec_itemids: NotRequired[Integer[Array, "n_trials num_recalled"]]
    irt: NotRequired[Integer[Array, "n_trials num_recalled"]]
    session: NotRequired[Integer[Array, "n_trials 1"]]

text:

Required Fields

Shape
Description




subject
(n_trials, 1)
Subject ID for each trial


listLength
(n_trials, 1)
Number of items presented in each trial


pres_itemnos
(n_trials, max_presented)
Within-list item positions (1-indexed, 0 = padding)

Recalled item positions (1-indexed, 0 = padding)





Optional Fields








Field
Shape
Description

irt
(n_trials, max_recalled)
Inter-response times

Session identifiers

Indexing Convention
All item indices are 1-indexed: - Position 1 = first item in the list - Position 0 = padding (unused entry)

title: Get Started
text:
Install the latest stable version using pip:
pip install jaxcmr
Or install the development version from GitHub:
pip install git+https://github.com/githubpsyche/jaxcmr


jaxcmr uses JAX for fast computation. By default, JAX runs on CPU. For GPU acceleration:
# CUDA (NVIDIA GPUs)
pip install -U "jax[cuda12]"

# Metal (Apple Silicon)
pip install jax-metal

title: jaxcmr
text: jaxcmr is a Python library for specifying and evaluating computational models of memory search. It leverages a compositional design and JAX’s support for easy just-in-time compilation to streamline model prototyping and simulation so researchers can explore more ideas in less time.

title: Architecture
text: jaxcmr models are composed from three core components, each defined by a protocol.

title: Architecture
text:
The MemorySearch Protocol
All memory search models implement the MemorySearch protocol:
class MemorySearch(Protocol):
    item_count: int
    is_active: Bool[Array, ""]
    recallable: Bool[Array, " item_count"]
    recall_total: Integer[Array, ""]

    def experience(self, choice: Int_) -> "MemorySearch": ...
    def start_retrieving(self) -> "MemorySearch": ...
    def retrieve(self, choice: Int_) -> "MemorySearch": ...
    def activations(self) -> Float[Array, " item_count"]: ...
    def outcome_probability(self, choice: Int_) -> Float[Array, ""]: ...
    def outcome_probabilities(self) -> Float[Array, " recall_outcomes"]: ...
This defines the interface for: - Study phase: experience() processes each study item - Transition: start_retrieving() switches to retrieval mode - Retrieval phase: retrieve() processes each recall, activations() and outcome_probability() compute retrieval probabilities

title: Architecture
text:
Component Protocols
Models are built from three component types:

Context
Maintains temporal context that drifts during study and retrieval:
class Context(Protocol):
    state: Float[Array, " context_feature_units"]
    initial_state: Float[Array, " context_feature_units"]
    size: int

    def integrate(self, context_input, drift_rate) -> "Context": ...


Memory
Stores and retrieves associations:
class Memory(Protocol):
    state: Float[Array, " input_size output_size"]

    def associate(self, in_pattern, out_pattern, learning_rate) -> "Memory": ...
    def probe(self, in_pattern) -> Float[Array, " output_size"]: ...


TerminationPolicy
Determines when to stop recalling:
class TerminationPolicy(Protocol):
    def stop_probability(self, model: "MemorySearch") -> Float[Array, ""]: ...

title: Architecture
text:
Composition
A CMR model combines these components:
MemorySearch
├── Context (temporal context state)
├── Memory (MFC: item → context)
├── Memory (MCF: context → item)
└── TerminationPolicy (when to stop)

title: Architecture
text:
Factory Functions
Factory protocols enable dependency injection:
class ContextCreateFn(Protocol):
    def __call__(self, item_count: int) -> Context: ...

class MemoryCreateFn(Protocol):
    def __call__(self, list_length, parameters, context) -> Memory: ...
This allows swapping implementations without changing model code.

title: Evaluation Pipeline
text: Model evaluation connects loss functions, fitting algorithms, and simulation.

title: Evaluation Pipeline
text:
Pipeline Overview
Dataset → LossFnGenerator → Loss Function → FittingAlgorithm → FitResult
                                ↑
                          TrialSimulator

title: Evaluation Pipeline
Evaluation Pipeline
text:
FittingAlgorithm
Runs optimization:
class FittingAlgorithm(Protocol):
    def __init__(
        self,
        dataset: RecallDataset,
        features: Optional[...],
        base_params: Mapping[str, Float_],
        model_factory: Type[MemorySearchModelFactory],
        loss_fn_generator: Type[LossFnGenerator],
        hyperparams: Optional[dict[str, Any]] = None,
    ): ...

    def fit(
        self,
        trial_mask: Bool[Array, " trials"],
        fit_to_subjects: bool = True,
    ) -> FitResult: ...

title: Evaluation Pipeline
Evaluation Pipeline
text:
FitResult
Fitting returns a structured result:
class FitResult(TypedDict):
    fixed: dict[str, float]        # Fixed parameters
    free: dict[str, list[float]]   # Free parameter bounds
    fitness: list[float]           # Fitness values
    fits: dict[str, list[float]]   # Optimized parameter values
    hyperparameters: dict[str, Any]
    fit_time: float

title: Evaluation Pipeline
text:
Typical Workflow
# 1. Load data
dataset = load_dataset("experiment.h5")

# 2. Configure fitting
algorithm = ScipyDE(
    dataset=dataset,
    base_params=DEFAULT_PARAMS,
    model_factory=CMRFactory,
    loss_fn_generator=RecallLikelihood,
    hyperparams={"bounds": param_bounds}
)

# 3. Run fitting
result = algorithm.fit(trial_mask, fit_to_subjects=True)

# 4. Inspect results
print(result["fits"])
print(result["fitness"])

title: Why JAX
text: jaxcmr uses JAX as its computational backend. Understanding a few JAX concepts helps you get the most from the library.

title: Why JAX
text:
Why JAX for Memory Modeling?
Memory models require:

Fast simulation - Thousands of trials to evaluate a parameter set
Parallel fitting - Multiple subjects, multiple models
Reproducibility - Identical random seeds yield identical results

JAX addresses all three through its core features.

title: Why JAX
text:
Key JAX Concepts

JIT Compilation
jax.jit compiles Python functions to optimized machine code:
import jax

@jax.jit
def simulate_trial(model, study_items):
    # This runs much faster after first call
    ...
jaxcmr models are designed to be JIT-compatible. Most functions work seamlessly with jit.


Vectorization with vmap
jax.vmap automatically vectorizes functions across an axis:
# Instead of looping over trials:
for trial in trials:
    result = simulate(trial)

# Vectorize:
results = jax.vmap(simulate)(trials)
This enables parallel simulation across trials and subjects.


Pure Functions
JAX functions must be pure—no side effects, same inputs always produce same outputs. This enables:

Reproducibility: Random state is explicit (PRNGKey)
Parallelism: No hidden state to synchronize
Caching: Results can be safely cached



PyTrees
JAX represents nested data structures (dicts, dataclasses) as “PyTrees”. jaxcmr models are PyTrees, making them compatible with JAX transformations:
# Models can be passed through jit, vmap, etc.
model = CMR.create(list_length=16, parameters=params)
jax.tree_util.tree_map(lambda x: x.shape, model)  # Inspect structure

title: Why JAX
text:
Practical Implications
When working with jaxcmr:

Use JIT for fitting - Wrap loss functions in jit for speed
Vectorize over trials - Use vmap instead of loops
Manage random state explicitly - Pass PRNGKey to functions that sample
Models are immutable - Methods return new model instances

title: Custom Analyses
text: jaxcmr analyses follow consistent patterns. You can create custom analyses that integrate with the library.

title: Custom Analyses
text:
Analysis Pattern
Most analyses follow this structure:
import jax.numpy as jnp
from jaxtyping import Array, Integer, Float

def my_analysis(
    recalls: Integer[Array, "trials max_recalls"],
    list_length: int,
    **kwargs
) -> Float[Array, "..."]:
    """
    Compute my custom analysis.

    Args:
        recalls: Recall sequences (1-indexed, 0 = padding)
        list_length: Number of items in each list
        **kwargs: Additional parameters

    Returns:
        Analysis results
    """
    # Your implementation here
    ...

title: Custom Analyses
text:
Example: First Recall Probability
Probability of recalling each serial position first:
def first_recall_probability(
    recalls: Integer[Array, "trials max_recalls"],
    list_length: int,
) -> Float[Array, "list_length"]:
    """Probability each position is recalled first."""
    # Get first recall for each trial
    first_recalls = recalls[:, 0]

    # Count how often each position is first
    counts = jnp.zeros(list_length)
    for pos in range(1, list_length + 1):
        counts = counts.at[pos - 1].set(jnp.sum(first_recalls == pos))

    # Normalize
    return counts / jnp.sum(counts)

title: Custom Analyses
text:
JIT Compatibility
For performance, ensure your analysis is JIT-compatible:
import jax

@jax.jit
def my_fast_analysis(recalls, list_length):
    ...
Avoid Python loops over data—use JAX operations instead.

title: Custom Analyses
text:
Vectorization
Use jax.vmap to apply analyses across subjects or conditions:
# Apply analysis to each subject separately
subject_results = jax.vmap(my_analysis)(subject_data)

title: Scoring Data
text: Before computing detailed analyses, you often need basic metrics about recall performance.

title: Scoring Data
text:
Basic Metrics

Recall Count
Number of correct recalls per trial:
import jax.numpy as jnp

def recall_count(recalls):
    """Count non-zero (valid) recalls per trial."""
    return jnp.sum(recalls > 0, axis=1)

counts = recall_count(dataset["recalls"])


Recall Probability
Proportion of items recalled:
def recall_probability(recalls, list_length):
    """Compute recall probability per trial."""
    counts = jnp.sum(recalls > 0, axis=1)
    return counts / list_length.squeeze()

title: Scoring Data
text:
Filtering Trials
You may want to filter trials by subject, session, or other criteria:
# Trials for subject 1
mask = dataset["subject"].squeeze() == 1
subject_recalls = dataset["recalls"][mask]

title: Scoring Data
text:
Next Steps
With data loaded and validated, you can compute:

Serial Position Curve - Recall by presentation position
Conditional Response Probability - Temporal contiguity

title: Serial Position Curve
text:
The Serial Position Curve (SPC) plots recall probability as a function of study position. It typically shows:

Primacy effect: Enhanced recall for early items
Recency effect: Enhanced recall for recent items
Asymmetry: Recency is often stronger than primacy in immediate free recall

title: Serial Position Curve
text:
The Serial Position Curve (SPC) plots recall probability as a function of study position. It typically shows:

Primacy effect: Enhanced recall for early items
Recency effect: Enhanced recall for recent items
Asymmetry: Recency is often stronger than primacy in immediate free recall

title: Serial Position Curve
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.analyses.spc import spc, plot_spc

# Compute SPC from recalls array
curve = spc(data["recalls"], list_length=16)

# Plot comparing model and data
plot_spc(
    datasets=[simulation, data],
    labels=["Model", "Data"],
)

title: CMR: Context Maintenance and Retrieval
text: CMR (Context Maintenance and Retrieval) is the foundational model in jaxcmr. It explains free recall through the interplay of drifting temporal context and item-context associations.

title: CMR: Context Maintenance and Retrieval
text:
The Core Mechanism
CMR explains why we remember some items and not others through a single idea: temporal context. When you study a list of words, each word updates a mental context that drifts over time. Later, when you try to recall, your current context acts as a cue—items associated with similar contexts are more likely to come to mind.
This produces the three hallmark effects of free recall:

Recency: Recent items share context with the retrieval cue
Primacy: Early items receive stronger encoding (attention-based)
Contiguity: Recalling an item reinstates its context, cueing neighbors

title: CMR: Context Maintenance and Retrieval
text:
Mathematical Specification

Representations
CMR uses three representations:







Representation
Description




\(\mathbf{f}_i\)
Item features (one-hot vectors, size = list length)


\(\mathbf{c}\)
Temporal context (unit-length vector, size = list length + 1)


\(M^{FC}\), \(M^{CF}\)
Associative memory matrices





Memory Initialization
Before the experiment, two memory matrices exist with pre-experimental associations:
Item-to-context memory (MFC): \[M^{FC}_{pre(ij)} = \begin{cases} 1 - \gamma & \text{if } i=j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\]
Each item links to a unique context unit. The parameter \(\gamma\) (learning rate) determines how much room remains for new associations.
Context-to-item memory (MCF): \[M^{CF}_{pre(ij)} = \begin{cases} \delta & \text{if } i=j \\ \alpha & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\]
Here \(\delta\) (item support) gives each item a baseline association with its context unit, while \(\alpha\) (shared support) provides uniform associations across all items.


Encoding Phase
When item \(i\) is studied:
1. Retrieve contextual input: \[\mathbf{c}^{IN}_i = M^{FC} \mathbf{f}_i\]
2. Update context (drift): \[\mathbf{c}_i = \rho_i \mathbf{c}_{i-1} + \beta_{enc} \mathbf{c}^{IN}_i\]
where \(\rho_i\) ensures unit length: \[\rho_i = \sqrt{1 + \beta_{enc}^2\left[(\mathbf{c}_{i-1} \cdot \mathbf{c}^{IN}_i)^2 - 1\right]} - \beta_{enc}(\mathbf{c}_{i-1} \cdot \mathbf{c}^{IN}_i)\]
3. Form new associations: \[\Delta M^{FC}_{ij} = \gamma \mathbf{f}_i \mathbf{c}_j\] \[\Delta M^{CF}_{ij} = \phi_i \mathbf{c}_i \mathbf{f}_j\]
4. Primacy gradient:
Early items receive stronger associations: \[\phi_i = \phi_s e^{-\phi_d(i-1)} + 1\]
where \(\phi_s\) is the primacy scale and \(\phi_d\) is the decay rate.


Retrieval Phase
1. Initialize retrieval context:
Before recalling, context drifts toward the start-of-list state: \[\mathbf{c}_{start} = \rho_{N+1} \mathbf{c}_N + \beta_{start} \mathbf{c}_0\]
2. Compute activations: \[\mathbf{a} = M^{CF} \mathbf{c}\]
3. Apply choice sensitivity: \[\mathbf{a}' = \mathbf{a}^{\tau}\]
where \(\tau\) (choice sensitivity) sharpens the competition between items.
4. Convert to probabilities: \[P(i) = (1-P_{stop})\frac{a'_i}{\sum_k a'_k}\]
5. Stop probability: \[P_{stop}(j) = \theta_s e^{j\theta_r}\]
where \(j\) is the output position, \(\theta_s\) is the stop probability scale, and \(\theta_r\) is the growth rate.
6. After recall:
When item \(i\) is recalled, its context is reinstated: \[\mathbf{c}^{IN} = M^{FC} \mathbf{f}_i\] \[\mathbf{c}_{new} = \rho \mathbf{c}_{old} + \beta_{rec} \mathbf{c}^{IN}\]
This reinstated context now cues the next recall, producing the contiguity effect.

Parameters








Parameter
Symbol
Description

Context integration during study


start_drift_rate
\(\beta_{start}\)
Context shift toward start at retrieval onset


recall_drift_rate
\(\beta_{rec}\)
Context integration after each recall


learning_rate
\(\gamma\)
MFC association strength


primacy_scale
\(\phi_s\)
Initial primacy boost


primacy_decay
\(\phi_d\)
Rate of primacy decay


shared_support
\(\alpha\)
Baseline MCF associations


item_support
\(\delta\)
Self-support in MCF


choice_sensitivity
\(\tau\)
Competition sharpness


stop_probability_scale

Base stopping probability


stop_probability_growth
\(\theta_r\)
Stopping growth rate



See Parameters for complete documentation.

title: CMR: Context Maintenance and Retrieval
text:
Usage
from jaxcmr.models.cmr import CMR

# Define parameters
params = {
    "encoding_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "start_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "recall_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "learning_rate": 0.5,
    "primacy_scale": 2.0,
    "primacy_decay": 0.8,
    "shared_support": 0.05,
    "item_support": 0.25,
    "choice_sensitivity": 0.6,
    "stop_probability_scale": 0.05,
    "stop_probability_growth": 0.2,
    "learn_after_context_update": True,
    "allow_repeated_recalls": False,
}

# Create model for a 16-item list
model = CMR(list_length=16, parameters=params)

# Study phase: present items 1-16
for item_id in range(1, 17):
    model = model.experience(item_id)

# Transition to retrieval
model = model.start_retrieving()

# Get probability of each outcome (stop + 16 items)
probs = model.outcome_probabilities()

# Simulate a recall
recalled_item = 16  # Last item (1-indexed)
model = model.retrieve(recalled_item)

# Get next outcome probabilities
probs = model.outcome_probabilities()

title: CMR: Context Maintenance and Retrieval
text:
How Context Creates Memory Effects

Recency Effect
At the end of study, context most closely matches the final items. The drift equation ensures that recent context inputs have the largest influence:
c_final = ρ₁₅c₁₄ + β·c¹⁵ᴵᴺ
        = ρ₁₅(ρ₁₄c₁₃ + β·c¹⁴ᴵᴺ) + β·c¹⁵ᴵᴺ
        = ...
Earlier items contribute exponentially less to the final context.


Contiguity Effect
When you recall item 8, its context is reinstated:
\[\mathbf{c}^{IN} = M^{FC} \mathbf{f}_8\]
This retrieved context contains traces from when item 8 was studied—particularly items 7 and 9, which shared similar contexts. When this reinstated context cues MCF, items 7 and 9 receive high activation.


Asymmetry
Forward transitions (e.g., 8→9) are more likely than backward transitions (8→7) because:

During study, context drifts forward
The reinstated context for item 8 resembles the context after item 8, not before
Thus item 9’s stored context has higher similarity than item 7’s

title: CMR: Context Maintenance and Retrieval
text:
Architecture
CMR combines three swappable components:
CMR(
    list_length=16,
    parameters=params,
    mfc_create_fn=LinearMemory.init_mfc,  # Item → Context
    mcf_create_fn=LinearMemory.init_mcf,  # Context → Item
    context_create_fn=TemporalContext.init,
    termination_policy_create_fn=PositionalTermination,
)
Different implementations can be substituted while maintaining the CMR interface. See Context and Memory for component details.

title: CMR: Context Maintenance and Retrieval
text:
References

Howard, M. W., & Kahana, M. J. (2002). A distributed representation of temporal context. Journal of Mathematical Psychology, 46, 269-299.
Polyn, S. M., Norman, K. A., & Kahana, M. J. (2009). A context maintenance and retrieval model of organizational processes in free recall. Psychological Review, 116(1), 129-156.
Morton, N. W., & Polyn, S. M. (2016). A predictive framework for evaluating models of semantic organization in free recall. Journal of Memory and Language, 86, 119-140.

title: Models
text: This section documents the memory search models available in jaxcmr. The library provides a unified framework for implementing and comparing different variants of the Context Maintenance and Retrieval (CMR) model.

title: Models
text:
Why Do We Model Memory?
Free recall experiments reveal robust patterns:

Recency: Items from the end of a list are recalled first and most often
Primacy: Items from the beginning are also recalled well
Contiguity: Recalled items tend to come from nearby serial positions
Forward asymmetry: Transitions favor items that followed the just-recalled item

CMR explains these patterns through a simple mechanism: temporal context that drifts during study and is reinstated during retrieval.

title: Models
text:
Model Architecture
A CMR model combines three core components:

Context: A temporal context representation that drifts over time
Memory: Associative stores (MFC and MCF) linking items and contexts
Termination Policy: Determines when recall stops

These components implement protocols defined in jaxcmr.typing, allowing different implementations to be swapped while maintaining compatibility with the rest of the library.

title: Models
text:
Baseline Models

CMR
The base Context Maintenance and Retrieval model. Start here to understand the core mechanism that all variants build upon.


Context
The TemporalContext component implements drifting context representations that form the heart of CMR’s temporal dynamics.


Memory
Linear (Hebbian) and Instance (MINERVA-2 style) memory implementations, with tradeoffs between event-blending and event-preserving storage.


Parameters
Complete reference for all CMR parameters, their meanings, and typical value ranges.

title: Models
text:
Repetition Variants
Models addressing how repeated items are encoded and retrieved:



Model
Description




Positional CMR
Position-based encoding creates distinct traces for repetitions


Drift Positional
Different drift rate for repeated items


Reinforcement
Reinforces first-presentation trace at repetition


Outlist CMR
Out-of-list context mixing for distinct repetition traces


Blend Positional
Hybrid item/position context streams


Distinct Contexts
Positional encoding with item-level recallability


No Reinstate
No context reinstatement during encoding

title: Models
text:
Semantic Variants
Models integrating pre-experimental semantic associations:



Model
Description




Additive Semantic
Semantic similarity as additive boost to activation


Multiplicative Semantic
Semantic as multiplicative gate (foraging-inspired)

title: Models
text:
CRU Variants
Models bridging Context Retrieval and Updating (CRU) with CMR architectures:



Model
Description




CRU Overview
Introduction to CRU-CMR bridge models


Omnibus CRU-CMR
Full CRU with item identification and serial position scaling


CMR Compare
CMR baseline for factorial comparison


Competitive Termination
Context-based termination as item competition

title: Models
text:
EEG Variants
Models incorporating neural signals (LPP) to modulate encoding:



Model
Description




EEG-eCMR Overview
Brain-based modulation of memory encoding


Emotional CMR
Emotional memory channel with LPP modulation


Strength Model
Baseline without context retrieval

title: Models
text:
Usage Example
from jaxcmr.models.cmr import CMR, make_factory
import jaxcmr.components.linear_memory as LinearMemory
import jaxcmr.components.context as TemporalContext
from jaxcmr.components.termination import PositionalTermination

# Create a model factory
Factory = make_factory(
    LinearMemory.init_mfc,
    LinearMemory.init_mcf,
    TemporalContext.init,
    PositionalTermination,
)

factory = Factory(dataset, features=None)

# Define parameters
params = {
    "encoding_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "start_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "recall_drift_rate": 0.5,
    "learning_rate": 0.5,
    "primacy_scale": 2.0,
    "primacy_decay": 0.8,
    "shared_support": 0.05,
    "item_support": 0.25,
    "choice_sensitivity": 1.0,
    "stop_probability_scale": 0.05,
    "stop_probability_growth": 0.2,
    "learn_after_context_update": True,
    "allow_repeated_recalls": False,
}

# Create model for a specific trial
model = factory.create_trial_model(trial_index=0, parameters=params)

# Study items (1-indexed)
for item in [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]:
    model = model.experience(item)

# Begin retrieval
model = model.start_retrieving()

# Get retrieval probabilities
probs = model.outcome_probabilities()

title: Models
text:
Choosing a Model

For Standard Free Recall
Start with the base CMR model. It captures recency, primacy, contiguity, and forward asymmetry with a minimal parameter set.


For Lists with Repeated Items
Consider Positional CMR or Blend Positional CMR. These handle the ambiguity of which presentation’s context to reinstate.


For Semantic Recall
Use Additive Semantic CMR for typical semantic effects, or Multiplicative Semantic CMR for foraging-inspired gating behavior.


For Serial Recall
The CRU variants are designed for serial recall with their item identification and position-based encoding mechanisms.


For Emotional Memory
The EEG-eCMR models allow brain activity (LPP) to modulate encoding strength, testing brain-behavior relationships.

title: Models
text:
Implementation Philosophy
All models in jaxcmr share a common interface defined in jaxcmr.typing.MemorySearch:

experience(item) — Process a study event
start_retrieving() — Transition to retrieval mode
retrieve(item) — Process a recall event
outcome_probabilities() — Get retrieval probability distribution

This allows models to be swapped in evaluation pipelines without changing the analysis code.

title: Parameters
text: This page documents all parameters used in jaxcmr CMR models, their meanings, and typical value ranges.

title: Parameters
text:
Context Drift Parameters
These control how the temporal context representation evolves.








Parameter
Description
Typical Range




encoding_drift_rate
How much context changes when studying each item. Higher values = faster drift, more temporal distinctiveness
0.3 - 0.9


start_drift_rate
Context drift at the transition from study to recall. Often similar to encoding drift rate
0.3 - 0.9


recall_drift_rate
Context drift after recalling each item. Controls how much recalled items reinstate their context
0.3 - 0.9



Intuition: With low drift rates, context changes slowly and distant items share similar contexts. With high drift rates, each item has a more distinct context, increasing temporal precision.

title: Parameters
text:
Primacy Parameters
These create the primacy effect (better memory for early list items).








Parameter
Description
Typical Range




primacy_scale
Multiplier for the first item’s learning rate. Higher = stronger primacy
1.0 - 5.0


primacy_decay
Exponential decay rate across serial positions. Higher = primacy effect decays faster
0.3 - 1.5



The learning rate for position \(i\) is:
\[\text{lr}_i = \text{primacy\_scale} \times e^{-\text{primacy\_decay} \times i}\]

title: Parameters
text:
Association Strength Parameters
These control the strength of item-context associations.








Parameter
Description
Typical Range




learning_rate
Base strength for new MFC associations (item → context)
0.3 - 1.0


shared_support
Pre-experimental MCF association strength (all context units → all items)
0.01 - 0.2


item_support
Additional MCF association strength for matching context-item pairs
0.1 - 0.5



Intuition: shared_support creates a baseline retrieval probability for all items. item_support gives each item extra support from its corresponding context unit.

title: Parameters
text:
Retrieval Parameters
These control the retrieval process.








Parameter
Description
Typical Range




choice_sensitivity
Softmax temperature for converting activations to probabilities. Higher = more deterministic (winner-take-all)
0.1 - 2.0



With choice_sensitivity = 0, retrieval is random among available items. As it increases, the most activated item becomes more likely to be retrieved.

title: Parameters
text:
Stopping Parameters
These control when recall terminates (using PositionalTermination).








Parameter
Description
Typical Range




stop_probability_scale
Base probability of stopping at first recall position
0.01 - 0.2


stop_probability_growth
Exponential growth rate of stopping probability
0.1 - 0.5



Stop probability at recall position \(i\) is:
\[P(\text{stop})_i = \text{scale} \times e^{\text{growth} \times i}\]

title: Parameters
text:
Boolean Parameters








Parameter
Description
Default




learn_after_context_update
If true, associations use the updated (post-drift) context state
Varies


allow_repeated_recalls
If true, items can be recalled multiple times
False

A complete parameter dictionary for CMR:

Different CMR variants may require additional parameters:

For models with semantic similarity:







Parameter
Description
